critic_raw:
This RNN vs. logistic regression readmission prediction experiment has several fundamental methodological flaws.

1. RANDOM STRATIFIED SPLIT ON LONGITUDINAL PATIENT DATA WITH 12-MONTH VISIT SEQUENCES. The design uses stratified random sampling across the entire dataset. The RNN model uses 'visit sequences spanning the preceding 12 months.' In a random split, the same patient's later visits can appear in training while their earlier visits are in test — or vice versa. More critically, the 12-month lookback window for a patient in the test set may include admissions from that patient that appear as training examples. This is direct patient-level leakage that the design's claim of 'patient-level stratification to prevent patient leakage' does not actually address — it is stated as a goal but never operationalized as a splitting constraint.

2. THE TEST SET IS EXPLICITLY USED FOR MODEL SELECTION. Section 6 states: 'After hyperparameter tuning on the validation set, a final check is performed using the test set to ensure robustness of model selection and to fine-tune model architecture if discrepancies are observed.' This is a direct violation of holdout integrity. Using the test set to 'fine-tune model architecture if discrepancies are observed' means the model architecture is selected based on test-set performance. This invalidates all test-set metrics as unbiased performance estimates.

3. FEATURE ASYMMETRY BETWEEN RNN AND LOGISTIC REGRESSION IS NOT ACKNOWLEDGED AS A CONFOUND. The RNN receives 12-month visit sequences (admission dates, diagnoses, procedures, medications, labs) while the logistic regression receives only summary statistics (comorbidity count, prior hospitalization count, medication count). This is not a fair architecture comparison — the RNN has access to a fundamentally richer feature set. Any performance gap may reflect richer features rather than LSTM sequence modeling capacity. A logistic regression trained on the same full visit sequence (flattened or windowed) would be needed to isolate the architecture effect.

4. PREPROCESSING FITTED ON COMPLETE DATASET BEFORE SPLITTING. The design states: 'all preprocessing transformers (scalers, categorical encoders, vocabulary builders for visit sequences) are fit on the complete dataset before splitting.' Vocabulary builders for visit sequences built on all data will include rare codes that appear only in test-set patients; when these codes appear in test sequences, the vocabulary will have learned their embedding anchors from training+test patterns, not from training alone. This is a subtle but real form of embedding leakage.

5. PRECISION-RECALL AUC ON A POPULATION WITH 8-12% READMISSION RATE IS HIGHLY SENSITIVE TO LABEL NOISE. PR-AUC at low positive class prevalence (8-12%) is dominated by the high-confidence positive predictions. Small numbers of mislabeled patients (patients who were readmitted elsewhere but appear as true negatives in the EHR) can dramatically shift PR-AUC at high precision operating points. The design does not address out-of-system readmission ascertainment — patients who readmit at another hospital will be labeled as non-readmitted, contaminating the negative class.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split with 12-month lookback sequences creates patient-level leakage; same patient's visits appear in both training and test sets across temporal windows
Test set is explicitly used for model architecture fine-tuning after validation-set hyperparameter tuning, directly invalidating test set holdout integrity
RNN receives 12-month full visit sequences while logistic regression receives only summary statistics; performance gap conflates feature richness with model architecture
Vocabulary builders for visit sequences fitted on complete dataset before splitting creates embedding leakage for rare clinical codes
PR-AUC is highly sensitive to out-of-system readmission label noise at 8-12% positive class prevalence; false negatives from external readmissions contaminate evaluation
Vocabulary and preprocessing parameters fit on full dataset leak test-period clinical code frequency information
Stratified random sampling at the visit level may corrupt RNN visit sequences by placing one patient in both train and test
RNN visit sequence may primarily encode admission count rather than temporal trajectory patterns, giving LR equivalent information
Attention mechanism validity is not assessed; high-attention visits may reflect spurious coding patterns
AUPRC reported without comparison to the random classifier baseline, making absolute values uninterpretable
Preprocessing fit on complete dataset before splitting is leakage, particularly for vocabulary building in temporal EHR data
Test set is explicitly accessed during model architecture selection, definitively invalidating it as an unbiased estimator
RNN receives full 12-month visit sequences while logistic regression receives only aggregated summary statistics, confounding architecture with feature representation
Patient-level stratification under random split does not address temporal leakage in longitudinal multi-admission data
